evidence: ExplorationHandlers → SemanticFieldExplorer seam is clean: handlers own HTTP parameter parsing and response assembly; explorer owns domain algorithm — no HTTP concerns leak into the explorer
ExploreResponseAssembler is the single translation point from model to JSON-ready maps — centralised, no scattered ad-hoc serialization in handler methods
ValidatedExploreRequest and ValidatedSingleSeedRequest records capture the HTTP→domain parameter translation boundary cleanly
GrammarConfig (value object) + GrammarConfigLoader (file I/O) separation makes the config loading seam explicit and testable via fromReader()

buildBaseExploreResponse in ExplorationHandlers builds a partial response map; callers then add seed/seeds-specific fields before calling ExploreResponseAssembler.populateExploreResponse — response assembly is spread across three call sites rather than one

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison Javadoc explains why it asymmetrically does not call buildExploreRequest — correct justification, but the asymmetry itself adds seam friction for a reader learning the handler protocol

ExploreResponseAssembler.populateComparisonResponse takes Set<String> seeds parameter AND accesses result.nouns() for the 'seeds' response field — same data concept enters from two different sources at the same boundary
SemanticFieldExplorer is a stable facade insulating the HTTP layer from multi-seed vs single-seed vs comparison algorithm distinctions — clean seam design

ConcordanceHandlers.parseConcordanceExamplesRequest uses a private ConcordanceExamplesRequest record to cleanly capture parsed parameters before handler logic — consistent with the ValidatedXxxRequest pattern in ExplorationHandlers

strengths: ExploreResponseAssembler provides a single, coherent model-to-presentation translation boundary with no scattered ad-hoc maps in handlers
ValidatedXxxRequest / XxxRequest private records capture the HTTP→domain translation at a well-defined point
GrammarConfigLoader.fromReader() provides a clean testability seam that avoids file-system coupling in tests
SemanticFieldExplorer facade insulates the HTTP layer from changes to the three algorithm implementations behind it
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: Seam design is mostly clean; the primary issues are spread response-assembly (base map built in one place, extended in another, populated in a third) and a data duplication where populateComparisonResponse receives seeds from two sources.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase handles most seams well — the ExploreResponseAssembler boundary is clean, handler-to-explorer communication is explicit, and the grammar config seam is well-designed. The score is held in the mid 70s because the explore response is assembled across multiple call sites (buildBaseExploreResponse + caller adds seed fields + populateExploreResponse), and the comparison handler's asymmetric protocol requires inline documentation to explain. These create non-trivial cognitive load when tracing the data flow for a new contributor.

issues.summary: Explore response map is assembled across three sites: buildBaseExploreResponse, caller-added seed fields, and ExploreResponseAssembler.populateExploreResponse
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExplore: Map<String, Object> response = buildBaseExploreResponse(...); response.put("seed", result.seed()); ExploreResponseAssembler.populateExploreResponse(response, result);

handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti: Map<String, Object> response = buildBaseExploreResponse(...); response.put("seeds", ...); response.put("seed_count", ...); ExploreResponseAssembler.populateExploreResponse(response, result);
The boundary between 'base', 'seed-specific', and 'exploration-data' fields is spread across ExplorationHandlers and ExploreResponseAssembler with no single assembly point
issues.suggestion: Consolidate all explore response assembly into ExploreResponseAssembler — add a buildSingleSeedResponse(ExplorationResult, ExploreParams) and buildMultiSeedResponse(ExplorationResult, ExploreParams) factory method in ExploreResponseAssembler, returning the complete response map. Handlers call a single method and get a complete, ready-to-send response.

issues.summary: ExploreResponseAssembler.populateComparisonResponse receives seeds from both the Set<String> parameter and result.nouns(), sourcing the same concept from two different paths
evidence: populateComparisonResponse signature: (Map<String,Object> response, ComparisonResult result, Set<String> seeds, int topCollocates, double minLogDice)
response.put("seeds", new ArrayList<>(result.nouns())) — uses result.nouns(), not the seeds parameter
response.put("seed_count", seeds.size()) — uses the seeds parameter, not result.nouns().size()
These two could diverge if result.nouns() is reordered or filtered vs. the seeds passed by the caller
issues.suggestion: Remove the Set<String> seeds parameter from populateComparisonResponse and derive seed count from result.nouns().size(). The method already has full information via the ComparisonResult parameter; the extra parameter creates a redundant and potentially inconsistent data path.

root_causes: Response assembly grew incrementally as new fields were added to explore endpoints without consolidating into ExploreResponseAssembler
